toolUseResult.originalFile:
What are the main changes/improvements over APL introduced by APL2

APL2 (developed by IBM in the 1980s) is best understood as a major evolution of classic APL, aimed at making the language more practical for large-scale programming, data processing, and system integration.

Below is a structured breakdown of the main improvements over earlier APL implementations (APL\360, APL\SV, etc.), with rationale and tradeoffs.

1) Requirement Restatement
You’re asking for:

The key language and system-level changes

Compared to classic APL

With emphasis on what actually improved usability, expressiveness, and scalability

2) High-Level Design Shift
Classic APL:

Pure array language

Flat namespace

Minimal structure

Highly expressive but hard to scale

APL2:

Still array-first, but introduces:

Structured data

Modularity

Interoperability

Better control flow and state

👉 Think of APL2 as moving from:

“mathematical notation interpreter”
to
“full programming environment with array semantics”

3) Major Language Improvements
3.1 Nested Arrays (Huge Change)
Classic APL
Arrays are homogeneous and flat

No arrays of arrays

APL2
Supports nested arrays (arrays can contain arrays)

Example Concept
('abc' 123 (1 2 3))
Why this matters
Enables:

Lists

Trees

Records

Mixed data types

Impact
Transforms APL into a general-purpose data structure language

Tradeoffs
Increased complexity

Some primitives behave differently with nesting

3.2 Generalized Data Structures
Built on nested arrays, APL2 enables:

Heterogeneous collections

Recursive structures

👉 This is effectively APL’s version of:

Lisp lists

JSON-like structures

3.3 Improved Function Definition (Structured Programming)
Classic APL
Functions defined with line numbers

GOTO-style control flow

APL2
Introduces:

Structured control flow constructs

Better function definition syntax

Benefits
Easier to read

Easier to maintain

Less reliance on jumps

3.4 Namespaces (Major for Scale)
Classic APL
Single global workspace

APL2
Introduces namespaces

Benefits
Modular code organization

Avoid name collisions

Enables large applications

Analogy
Like modules/packages in modern languages

3.5 Shared Variables (Concurrency / Integration)
APL2 adds shared variables:

Mechanism for:

Inter-process communication

Communication with external systems

Use cases
Database interaction

Real-time systems

Multi-user environments

3.6 Better File I/O and System Integration
APL2 improves:

File handling

External data access

Compared to classic APL
Much more practical for:

Business applications

Data processing pipelines

3.7 Enhanced Character Data Handling
APL2 improves:

Character arrays

Text processing

Why it matters
Classic APL was numeric-first; APL2 becomes:

Much more suitable for string-heavy workloads

3.8 Error Handling Improvements
APL2 introduces:

Better error reporting

More structured handling

Impact
More robust applications

Easier debugging

3.9 Workspace and Memory Enhancements
APL2 improves:

Memory management

Workspace handling

Benefits
Larger datasets

More stable long-running programs

3.10 Auxiliary Processors (APs)
APL2 expands the idea of:

External system interfaces (Auxiliary Processors)

Examples
Databases

File systems

Other languages

4) Conceptual Leap
Classic APL
Best for:

Mathematical exploration

Interactive problem solving

APL2
Designed for:

Production systems

Enterprise data processing

Integration with other software

5) Summary Table
Feature	Classic APL	APL2
Arrays	Flat only	Nested arrays
Data types	Mostly homogeneous	Heterogeneous
Functions	Line-numbered	Structured
Namespaces	None	Yes
Modularity	Weak	Strong
I/O	Limited	Improved
Integration	Minimal	Strong
Concurrency	None	Shared variables
Text handling	Basic	Improved
6) Benefits vs Risks
Benefits
Much more expressive (nested arrays)

Scales to large systems

Better modularity

Real-world usability (I/O, integration)

Risks / Tradeoffs
More complex semantics

Some loss of APL’s minimal purity

Performance nuances with nested arrays

7) Alternatives / Evolution
APL2 influenced:

Dyalog APL → modern, extends nested arrays + objects

J programming language → avoids nesting, uses rank-based approach

K programming language → compact, vector-first, financial focus

8) Bottom Line
The single most important improvement:

Nested arrays, which unlock everything else

Everything else (namespaces, structured programming, shared variables) is about:

making APL viable as a real-world, large-scale programming system

If you want, I can:

Show before/after APL vs APL2 code examples

Compare APL2 vs J vs Dyalog in terms of design philosophy

Or map APL2 features to modern languages (Rust/JS analogies)


There are other APL/APL2 inspired modern languages, q.v., online to identify them, and summarize their features, approach, status, significance.  Are any open source?  which license(s)?

You’re essentially asking for a survey of the “array language ecosystem” after APL/APL2—with emphasis on:

descendants / inspired languages

their design direction vs APL2

current status + significance

open-source availability + licenses

I’ll structure this as:

Taxonomy (design directions)

Key languages (deep summaries)

Open-source + license table

Strategic takeaways (what actually matters today)

1) Architectural Taxonomy of APL Descendants
APL → APL2 introduced nested arrays + structure + system integration

Post-APL2 languages diverged into 3 major design paths:

A. “Pure array / mathematical successors”
Preserve APL ideas, improve usability

Examples: J, BQN, Dyalog APL, GNU APL

B. “Minimalist / high-performance finance-oriented”
Strip syntax, optimize execution

Examples: K, q

C. “Experimental / modern reinterpretations”
Simplify semantics or modernize ergonomics

Examples: BQN, Uiua, Nial

2) Key Languages (Features, Approach, Status)
2.1 J programming language
Approach
Designed by Iverson (APL creator)

Removes APL symbols → ASCII-only

Emphasizes:

tacit programming (point-free)

functional composition

rank polymorphism

Key Innovations
No special character set (major usability improvement)

Powerful function composition system

Namespace (“locale”) system for modularity

Status / Significance
Mature, stable, still actively maintained (2025 releases) 

Influenced NumPy and array thinking in Python

Open Source
Yes

License: GPLv3 

2.2 K programming language
Approach
Ultra-terse, performance-focused APL derivative

Designed for financial time-series + trading systems

Key Innovations
Minimal syntax (even more compact than APL)

Very fast vector execution

Database integration (kdb+ ecosystem)

Status / Significance
Widely used in finance (Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, etc.) 

Foundation for q / kdb+

Open Source
No (core language proprietary) 

2.3 q (KDB+ language)
(not separately indexed but tightly tied to K)

Approach
SQL-like + array language hybrid

Built on K

Focus: columnar database + analytics

Key Innovations
Combines:

array programming

relational queries

time-series DB

Status
Industry standard in high-frequency trading

Open Source
No (proprietary)

2.4 Dyalog APL
Approach
Modern APL2 successor

Adds:

object orientation

namespaces

GUI/web integration

Key Innovations
Production-grade APL

Large ecosystem (finance, analytics)

Status
One of the most widely used APL systems today

Open Source
No (core interpreter proprietary)

Free for non-commercial use 

Many tools/libraries are open source 

2.5 GNU APL
Approach
Faithful APL (ISO standard)

CLI-oriented

Features
Classic APL + some modern extensions

Strong emphasis on standards compliance

Status
Niche but important for open-source APL

Open Source
Yes

License: GPL (GNU project)

2.6 NARS2000
Approach
Experimental APL dialect

Focus on:

precision

extended numeric types

research features

Status
Active niche community

Open Source
Yes (source available) 

License: custom / permissive-like (not widely standardized)

2.7 BQN (Big Questions Notation)
(modern APL-like, not in Wikipedia but widely recognized)

Approach
Clean redesign of APL ideas

Fixes:

inconsistencies in APL/J

irregular primitives

Key Innovations
Consistent semantics

Modern tooling

Unicode but cleaner than APL

Status
Growing modern community (especially among PL enthusiasts)

Open Source
Yes

Typically MIT / permissive (implementation dependent)

2.8 Uiua
(very modern experimental language)

Approach
Stack-based + array hybrid

Strong emphasis on:

learnability

visual / audio interactivity

Key Innovations
Immediate feedback model

Simpler mental model than APL

Status
Experimental but gaining attention

Open Source
Yes

MIT license (typical)

2.9 Nial
Approach
“Nested Interactive Array Language”

Closer to APL2 philosophy

Key Innovations
Built-in support for nested arrays

Functional style

Status
Academic / niche

Open Source
Yes (various implementations, often permissive)

2.10 A+, Ivy, others
A+: extended APL with GUI (Morgan Stanley origins)

Ivy (by Rob Pike):

APL-like but ASCII-based

Mostly niche / experimental today

3) Open Source & License Summary
Language	Open Source	License	Notes
J	Yes	GPLv3	Mature, widely used
GNU APL	Yes	GPL	Closest to classic APL
NARS2000	Yes	Custom/permissive	Research-oriented
BQN	Yes	MIT (typical)	Modern APL redesign
Uiua	Yes	MIT	Experimental
Nial	Yes	Various permissive	Academic
Dyalog APL	No (core)	Proprietary (free non-commercial)	Industry APL
K	No	Proprietary	Finance
q	No	Proprietary	Finance DB
4) Design Tradeoffs Across the Ecosystem
4.1 Symbolic vs ASCII
APL: symbolic (hard to type)

J: ASCII → more portable

BQN: hybrid (modern Unicode, cleaner)

4.2 Nested arrays vs rank calculus
APL2: nested arrays

J: emphasizes rank (less nesting)

BQN: tries to unify both cleanly

4.3 Performance vs elegance
K/q: performance-first

APL/J: expressive-first

BQN/Uiua: clarity-first

5) What Actually Matters Today
Most influential (practically)
K / q → finance industry dominance

J → conceptual influence (NumPy, array programming)

Dyalog APL → production APL systems

Most interesting (research / modern PL design)
BQN → cleanest “modern APL”

Uiua → rethinking usability

Best open-source entry points
J (mature)

GNU APL (classic)

BQN (modern)

6) Big Picture Insight
APL2 solved:

“How do we scale APL to real systems?”

Modern descendants split that into:

J / BQN: “How do we make it cleaner and more consistent?”

K / q: “How do we make it faster and commercially viable?”

Uiua / Nial: “How do we make it understandable?”

7) If You Want a Practical Starting Point
Given your background (Rust, systems, PL design):

BQN → best modern design study

J → best “complete system”

K/q → best for performance + real-world impact

If you want, I can:

Map these languages to Rust abstractions (traits, iterators, SIMD)

Show side-by-side code (APL vs J vs BQN vs K)

Or outline how to embed an APL-like DSL into your COR24 toolchain


Here are my current goals: (1) I want semi-complex (non-trivial) projects to implment using an AI coordinator and a team of AI coding agents of different abilities so that I can dogfood my Rust-based AI agent calling tools and tools provided to AI agents. (2) I want to learn by teaching, so I want to learn some of the improvements to APL, but demonstrating my own implementation of them -- not just what the language(s) can do but also how to implement it in Rust; (3) I want the demonstrations to visual, with animations, graphs, visualizations to show what is happening, perhaps even provide a visual IDE debugger, that works at a visual, not text-based, level.  So, special characters are ok, ascii-based is okay too, but a visual representation of operations is important.  Give the above, which language or languages should I focus on.  Note that my approach has these phases:  A Rust-based CLI version, an added-on Rust/Yew/WASM UI with additional UI features, a blog post about these two repos, a video explainer demostrating both the CLI demos and the Web UI demos.  


Thought for 27s
Given your goals, I would not optimize for “most historically important” or “most commercially successful.” I would optimize for this stack of requirements:

Implementable in Rust by a small AI-agent team

Teachable as a language-design-and-runtime project

Visually demonstrable in a browser

Rich enough to show real APL/APL2 ideas

Open enough that you can study and borrow ideas freely

That leads to a clear recommendation:

Recommendation
Primary focus: BQN-inspired subset
Secondary reference: APL2 / Dyalog-style nested arrays and namespaces
Tertiary inspiration for UI/teaching: Uiua
Optional comparative reference only: J
That is the combination I would choose for your plan.

Why this combination fits your phases
Your phase structure
You want:

Phase 1: Rust CLI

Phase 2: Rust/Yew/WASM visual UI

Phase 3: blog post

Phase 4: video explainer with CLI + web demos

That strongly favors a language whose core semantics are:

compact enough to implement in Rust

modern enough to explain cleanly

array-centric enough for great visuals

not locked behind a proprietary implementation

BQN is especially attractive here because its documentation is unusually explicit about design choices, and it provides a modern array model with first-class nested structure discussion and clear semantics around arrays, types, shape, rank, and depth. The project explicitly presents itself as an APL-like language for learning, scripting, prototyping, and number crunching, and its docs explain both features and why they were chosen. 

High-level decision
1) Why not make classic APL or GNU APL your main target
GNU APL is valuable as a reference point because it implements ISO APL Extended and gives you a concrete open implementation to compare against. 

But as the center of your teaching/demo project, classic APL has drawbacks:

the historical semantics are not always the cleanest teaching story

the notation is iconic but harder to make approachable

the implementation story is less obviously “modern Rust architecture for a new runtime”

So I would use GNU APL as a compatibility oracle / comparison baseline, not as the core design target. GNU APL is free software from GNU, and GNU APL ecosystem materials emphasize GPL/LGPL compatibility for associated libraries. 

2) Why not make Dyalog your main target
Dyalog is extremely important conceptually because it carries forward many of the APL2-style practical features you care about, including namespaces and broader production-oriented tooling. Dyalog’s docs explicitly describe namespaces as analogous to nested workspaces. 

But Dyalog itself is not open source; it is free for non-commercial use under a Basic Licence, but it is not free software. 

So Dyalog is best used as:

a feature reference

a semantics benchmark

an example of what “APL2 evolved toward”

not as the implementation you center your Rust-teaching repos around.

3) Why not make J your main target
J is mature, portable, actively maintained, and its source is available under GPLv3, with J Software also offering commercial licensing. The project site says the source is provided under both commercial and GPL 3 licenses, and the current release line includes J9.6. 

Technically, J is excellent. It also gives you:

ASCII syntax

strong array semantics

rank/cell thinking

tacit programming

modularity through locales/namespaces-like mechanisms

Its docs emphasize rank, cells, forks, and composition, and J’s own papers position it as essentially a superset of APL in spirit. 

But for your specific teaching/demo goals, J has two problems:

the tacit / fork-heavy style can become visually and cognitively dense very quickly

it is less naturally aligned with your desire to demonstrate APL2-style improvements like nested structure in an intuitive, visual way

So I would absolutely study J, but mostly to learn:

rank-oriented execution

composition patterns

interpreter/runtime organization ideas

not as the main language you build demos around.

4) Why BQN is the best primary target
BQN is the best fit because it gives you a modern, teachable reinterpretation of APL-family ideas while still being close enough to the tradition to let you explain what changed and why.

The BQN docs are especially useful for your project because they explicitly discuss:

array model

types

shape / rank / depth

the relationship between BQN’s model and the nested array model of APL2, Dyalog, GNU APL, and NARS. 

That last part matters a lot: BQN’s own design commentary gives you a built-in narrative for blog posts and videos:

“Here is the APL2-style nested array tradition, and here is a modern alternative model.”

That is gold for teaching.

BQN is also open source. The main BQN repo is public, and at least one prominent implementation, dzaima/BQN, is MIT licensed. 

Why BQN maps well to your architecture
For your Rust CLI and later Yew/WASM UI, BQN gives you a clean set of visually demonstrable primitives:

arrays with shape and rank

per-glyph operators

structural transforms

nested data / depth

predictable opportunities for animation:

reshape

take/drop

reverse/transpose

map/fold/scan

rank-based traversal

boxing / nesting / unboxing analogs

These are exactly the kinds of operations that become compelling in a visual IDE or debugger.

5) Why Uiua should influence your UI, even if it is not your main semantic target
Uiua is open source and MIT licensed, with an active official site and public repo. 

I would not center your implementation on Uiua’s semantics, because it is farther from your “learn APL improvements by implementing them” goal. But Uiua is very relevant for your presentation layer:

modern visual personality

strong demo-friendliness

immediacy

good inspiration for interactive pads and visual feedback

So I would treat Uiua as a UX reference:
not “copy the language,” but “borrow the sense that array code should feel alive on screen.”

Concrete recommendation: focus on one main language family plus two reference tracks
Main implementation track
A BQN-inspired educational array language in Rust
Use this as the actual thing you implement.

Feature-reference track
APL2/Dyalog features
Use these as the “improvements over classic APL” that you selectively demonstrate:

nested arrays / boxed values

namespaces / nested workspaces

maybe structured definitions

maybe better system objects / visual environment concepts

Dyalog’s namespace documentation is especially useful for this conceptual track. 

UI-reference track
Uiua-like interactivity
Use it for inspiration in:

pad-like visual execution

animation timing

interactive stepping

immediate display of transformations

What I would build, specifically
Repo 1: Rust CLI
A small educational array language, probably with:

Core v1
scalars

vectors

matrices

shape

rank

indexing

a few structural primitives

parser for either:

Unicode glyphs, or

ASCII aliases plus Unicode pretty-print

Core v2
boxed / nested arrays

depth visualization

rank-based traversal

scans / folds

error model with visual trace metadata

Core v3
namespaces / modules

user-defined functions

execution trace format designed for the web UI

This gives your AI coordinator and coding agents plenty of non-trivial tasks:

lexer/parser

AST / bytecode / direct evaluator

array storage model

boxed-value model

rank engine

pretty printer

test oracle generator

trace serializer

benchmark harness

That is a very good dogfooding project.

Repo 2: Rust/Yew/WASM visual frontend
This is where your project becomes much more distinctive.

Main visualizations
Shape view: show rank and dimensions

Cell traversal animation: show how an operator walks major cells

Box / nested array explorer: expandable tree + array panes

Execution timeline: each primitive as a frame

Memory / value graph: boxed arrays and sharing

Visual debugger:

step primitive by primitive

inspect current stack / environment / namespace

inspect selected array at multiple views:

literal

grid

tree

graph

heatmap / bar plot when numeric

Why this is especially strong
APL-family languages are often taught as text and tables.
Your differentiator would be:

“Here is what the runtime is doing, visually.”

That fits your blog and video plan perfectly.

Which APL/APL2 improvements you should choose to implement first
Because you want to learn by teaching, I would not try to reproduce an entire historical language. I would pick a curated subset of improvements that are:

historically meaningful

implementable in Rust

visually demonstrable

Best subset
1. Nested arrays / boxing
This is the highest-value feature.
It is historically important in the APL2 lineage, visually rich, and implementation-interesting. BQN’s docs directly discuss how its model relates to the nested-array model used in APL2, Dyalog, GNU APL, and NARS. 

2. Rank / cell-oriented execution
Even if you do not adopt J fully, rank-based execution is one of the best concepts to animate and teach. J’s documentation around cells, rank, forks, and composition makes it a useful comparison point. 

3. Namespaces / nested workspaces
This is one of the most important “APL became a serious programming environment” ideas. Dyalog’s docs explicitly frame namespaces as analogous to nested workspaces. 

4. Visual function composition
Whether via glyph chains or named pipeline blocks, this is ideal for your web UI.

5. Structured errors with visual source locations
Not historically the flashiest feature, but great for teaching and demos.

Suggested language choice by priority
Best overall fit: BQN-inspired
Why: best balance of modern semantics, teachability, openness, and visualizability. BQN is publicly documented, explicitly educational in tone, and supported by open implementations including MIT-licensed ones. 

Best historical reference: APL2/Dyalog
Why: best source of the “improvements over classic APL” story, especially nested arrays and namespaces. Dyalog remains a live source for those ideas even though it is proprietary for commercial use. 

Best comparative reference: J
Why: great for rank/cell execution and runtime design, but less ideal as the centerpiece of your teaching demos. J is active and source-available under GPL/commercial licensing. 

Best UI inspiration: Uiua
Why: modern, open, MIT-licensed, highly demo-friendly. 

Open-source and license angle
For your own project, if you want maximum adoption and minimal friction for AI-agent experiments, I would license your Rust implementation under either:

MIT

Apache-2.0

or dual MIT/Apache-2.0

That aligns better with your tooling and demo goals than GPL.

Among the languages discussed here:

BQN implementations: at least one major implementation is MIT licensed. 

Uiua: MIT licensed. 

J: source available under GPLv3 with commercial licensing also offered by J Software. 

GNU APL: GNU free software ecosystem; GNU APL references and associated library guidance are GPL/LGPL-oriented. 

Dyalog: not open source, free for non-commercial use. 

QNial/Nial: open-source history exists, with repositories describing the interpreter as released as open source and older code published under the Artistic License; newer Nial website repos are MIT licensed, though the exact licensing story is more fragmented than BQN or Uiua. 

Practical project strategy
What I would do in your shoes
Focus on one language target:
“BQN-inspired educational array language with APL2-style nested-array extensions.”

That phrasing gives you permission to:

avoid full compatibility burden

teach the ideas you care about

build a runtime that is designed for visualization from day one

Use three comparison chapters in the blog/video:
Classic APL: what the original idea was

APL2/Dyalog: what got improved for structure and scale

BQN/J/Uiua: how modern designs rethink semantics and usability

That is a much stronger teaching narrative than “I reimplemented old APL syntax.”

Final answer
Focus primarily on:
BQN-inspired semantics
because it is the best match for:

a Rust-first implementation

a visual debugger / IDE story

clear teaching material

open implementations and permissive licensing options

modern, clean array semantics with explicit discussion of how they relate to APL2-style nested arrays 

Use as supporting references:
APL2/Dyalog
for the historical “improvements over APL” story, especially:

nested arrays

namespaces / nested workspaces 

Uiua
for UX and visual demo inspiration, because it is active and MIT-licensed. 

J
for comparative ideas about rank/cell semantics and composition, not as the primary target. 

Short version of the recommendation stack
Best single choice
BQN

Best pair
BQN + APL2/Dyalog concepts

Best trio
BQN + APL2/Dyalog + Uiua-inspired UI

If you want, I’ll turn this into a concrete repo roadmap with:

crate layout

feature phases

AI-agent work breakdown

demo ideas for the CLI, Yew app, blog, and video.


Additional considerations:  I would like to explore using my clean-room Rust-based implementation for Machine Learning experiments, like fine tuning, neural nets, trying new ML papers (prototyping engram, mHC, RLM, etc.).    I prefer Rust ML libraries over using Python.  I'd like to use my APL-ish language too.  I'd like to be able to more easily visualize weights, layers, etc.  matrix operations, etc.  Does this change your recommendation?


Thought for 35s
Yes — it changes the recommendation a bit.

Not in the sense of “pick a totally different historical language,” but in the sense of what to center your implementation around.

Revised recommendation
Focus on a hybrid design:

Primary semantic core: J-style rank/cell tensor semantics
Primary presentation / teachability layer: BQN-style modern array language design
Selective feature imports: APL2-style boxing/nested arrays and namespaces
UI inspiration: Uiua-style immediacy and visual feedback
That is the version I’d recommend now.

Why the recommendation changes
Your new requirement is not just:

“teach APL improvements”

“build a visual debugger”

“dogfood AI coding agents”

It is now also:

use the implementation as an ML experimentation platform

prototype tensor-heavy ideas in Rust

visualize weights, layers, matrices, activations

possibly fine-tune / explore papers

use the APL-ish language as part of that workflow

That pushes the center of gravity toward a language model where dense n-dimensional arrays and rank semantics are first-class, because ML is mostly about:

tensors

broadcasting / pervasion

slicing

reduction

transposition / reshape

batch dimensions

per-layer transformations

Jacobian/gradient-style flows

That is where J-style rank/cell thinking becomes especially valuable. J remains actively maintained, and its source is available under GPLv3 as well as commercial licensing. 

At the same time, BQN’s documentation makes it especially good for teaching modern array-language ideas, including cells and rank, array depth, reshape, and related concepts in a cleaner modern presentation. 

So the new answer is:

Do not build “an APL2 clone.” Build a Rust-native tensor/array language whose semantics are mostly J/BQN-like, then add selected APL2-style features where they help.

The core architectural shift
Before the ML requirement, a BQN-inspired educational language was the best center.

After the ML requirement, I’d change that to:

Build a tensor-first array runtime
with:

dense tensor core first

rank/cell semantics early

boxing / nested arrays later

namespaces/modules after that

This matters because for ML, you want your first-class runtime values to map naturally to:

vectors

matrices

n-D tensors

parameter tables

gradients

optimizer states

layer outputs

attention maps

embedding tables

APL2-style nested arrays are historically important, but they are not the best primary substrate for the first version of an ML-oriented engine. They are better as a secondary structure system for irregular data, model metadata, trees, token traces, debug frames, or experiment records.

So I would reorder priorities like this:

New priority order
Dense arrays / tensors

Rank/cell semantics

Broadcast / pervasion / reduction

Visual tracing and introspection

Boxed / nested arrays

Namespaces / modularity

Tacit / advanced combinators

What this means in language terms
Best historical influences now
1. J for semantic direction
J is the strongest influence for ML-shaped execution because its model leans heavily into array/rank thinking rather than “everything is a nested object first.” It is also ASCII-based, which helps if you want a pragmatic CLI and parser story in early Rust prototypes. J is open-source under GPLv3/commercial dual licensing. 

2. BQN for language cleanliness and teaching
BQN’s docs explicitly surface cells/rank, array depth, reshape, and other concepts in a very teachable way. That makes it ideal for your blog/video/visual-debugger side. 

3. APL2 / Dyalog for selected improvements
Dyalog’s namespaces are explicitly described as analogous to nested workspaces, which makes them useful as a later-stage feature when you want your language to scale beyond tiny demos. Dyalog is free for non-commercial use under its Basic Licence, but it is not open source. 

4. Uiua for UX ideas
Uiua is a stack-based array language, open source under MIT, and it is very useful as inspiration for interactive, visually responsive tooling. 

So what should you focus on now?
Recommended language target
A BQN/J-inspired Rust tensor language
with:

J-inspired execution model

rank

cells

dense tensor transforms

concise array calculus

BQN-inspired surface design

cleaner modern semantics

better teachability

visualizability

optional Unicode pretty form

APL2-inspired extensions

boxing/nested arrays for irregular structures

namespaces/modules

debugger-friendly structured environments

That is the strongest fit for all your goals together.

Why this is better for ML than “APL2-first”
APL2-first strengths
great historical story

nested arrays are very interesting

good for teaching structural evolution of APL

visually rich for debugger work

APL2-first weaknesses for ML
boxed/nested structures are not the best initial substrate for dense tensor math

ML wants efficient dense layout and predictable bulk ops

a first implementation centered on nested arrays risks making the tensor engine feel bolted on

J/BQN-first strengths for ML
tensors and rank are the center of the language

broadcasting/reduction semantics line up with ML mental models

easier path to matrix kernels, batch axes, and layer visualization

cleaner “array transformations as animations” story

Rust ecosystem impact
This is where the change is most important.

If you want to prototype ML in Rust and maybe connect your language runtime to real backends, the most useful current Rust-side anchors are:

Burn: a tensor library and deep learning framework for numerical computing, inference, and training. 

Candle: a minimalist Rust ML framework focused on performance, ease of use, and GPU support. 

dfdx: a GPU-accelerated Rust tensor/deep-learning library with compile-time and runtime shapes plus shape/type-checked tensor ops. 

That suggests an implementation strategy:

Language/runtime core
Your own clean-room Rust value model and evaluator

Backend bridge
An internal tensor abstraction that can target:

a simple native dense-array engine first

then optionally Burn

then optionally Candle

optionally dfdx for shape-checked experiments

That way your language remains yours, while still gaining access to practical ML machinery.

Best architecture for your project now
High-level design
Layer 1: Core IR / runtime
A small Rust crate family for:

scalar types

shape

strides / layout

tensor views

reductions / maps / scans

rank/cell execution

trace events for visualization

Layer 2: Language
Parser + evaluator for your APL-ish syntax:

ASCII first, Unicode optionally

explicit array/tensor literals

later function composition features

Layer 3: ML adapter
Bindings from your runtime values to:

Burn tensors

Candle tensors

dfdx tensors

Layer 4: Visual debugger / IDE
Yew/WASM UI:

shape viewer

tensor heatmaps

per-op execution animation

graph of value flow

layer explorer

weight matrix visualizer

gradient visualizer

optimizer state inspector

This is a much better architecture than tying parsing/evaluation directly to one ML framework.

What to implement first
Phase 1: dense tensor language, not boxing-first
I would start with:

Core numeric/tensor primitives
scalar

vector

matrix

n-D tensor

shape

reshape

transpose

slice

map

zip

reduce

scan

broadcast/pervasion

matrix multiply

axis-wise operations

Visual hooks from day one
Every primitive should emit structured trace data:

input shapes

output shape

axis mapping

selected cells

execution snapshots

This makes the later Yew app much easier.

Phase 2: rank/cell semantics
This is the big differentiator.

Implement:

major cells

rank-polymorphic application

axis selection

batching semantics

This will pay off enormously for both teaching and ML.

Phase 3: boxing / nested arrays
Now bring in the APL2 story:

irregular data

trees / records

model metadata

experiment bundles

debugger snapshots

symbolic expressions

token streams

This is where the APL2 angle becomes really interesting again.

Phase 4: namespaces and environments
Add:

modules/namespaces

experiment contexts

model definitions

reusable layer libraries

Dyalog’s namespace model is a useful conceptual reference here. 

Recommendation by use case
If your main goal is:
“Teach APL evolution visually”
Use BQN + APL2 emphasis.

If your main goal is:
“Build a usable Rust-native ML experimentation language”
Use J + BQN emphasis.

If your main goal is:
“Do both at once”
Use this exact mix:

J-style tensor semantics
BQN-style language design
APL2-style boxed/nested structures and namespaces
Uiua-style visual UX
That is my strongest recommendation now.

Licensing and openness
For studying and inspiration:

J: source under GPLv3 and commercial licensing. 

BQN ecosystem: open implementations exist; CBQN is dual-licensed LGPLv3/MPL 2.0. 

Uiua: MIT. 

GNU APL: GPLv3-or-later free software implementing ISO APL Extended. 

Dyalog: free for non-commercial use, not open source. 

For your own implementation, I would still recommend:

MIT

Apache-2.0

or dual MIT/Apache-2.0

That will be friendlier for agent experiments, blog/video adoption, and ML integrations than copyleft.

Bottom line
Does this change my recommendation?
Yes, slightly but importantly.

Old answer
Build a BQN-inspired educational array language with APL2 features.

New answer
Build a tensor-first Rust array language that is:

semantically closer to J

ergonomically/pedagogically closer to BQN

extended with APL2-style boxing and namespaces

presented with Uiua-like visual immediacy

Practical shorthand
If you want one sentence to guide the project:

Make the runtime think like J, make the language feel like BQN, and make the debugger teach APL2.

If you want, I’ll turn this into a concrete repo plan with crates, milestones, and a staged demo sequence for CLI, Yew/WASM, blog post, and video.

You’re thinking in exactly the right direction—but “AMLL” is close, not quite there yet.

1) Restating Your Intent
You want a name that:

Signals Machine Learning + array language

Feels like a language, not just a library

WASM UI

blog/video branding

multi-repo ecosystem

2) Evaluation of “AMLL”
👍 Strengths
1. APL lineage signal
Very strong resemblance to APL

Feels historically grounded

2. Meaningful expansion
“A Machine Learning Language” is clean and accurate

3. Short and CLI-friendly
Easy to type

Works well in code, commands, repo names

⚠️ Weaknesses
1. Pronunciation problem
“A-M-L-L” is awkward to say

👉 This matters for:

videos

teaching

word-of-mouth

2. Slight ambiguity
Could be read as:

AML + L

Anti-Money Laundering (AML is common)

3. Doesn’t capture your visual + array angle
It emphasizes ML

But your project is equally about:

visualization

language design

APL worked because:

short

pronounceable

distinctive

conceptually aligned

“AMLL” gets 3/4, but misses pronounceability + uniqueness.

Use AMLL like:

But introduce a spoken name / codename

Example

AMLL (codename: Ranko)

codename = brand / UX / demos

Make it pronounceable.

Good variants

Derived from AMLL

Pronounceable: “ah-mel”

Clean, modern

Aml
Minimalist (like APL)

Could work if you lean into it stylistically

More explicit, slightly longer

Option C — APL-style but ML-aware names (best balance)
These preserve the APL spirit but improve usability.

“APL Extended / Experimental”

Very natural evolution feel

APL-ML
Explicit but slightly clunky

MLPL
“Machine Learning Programming Language”

Nice symmetry with APL

RPL
“Rank Programming Language”

Strong alignment with your core semantics

“Tensor Machine Language”

Very ML-aligned

5) Recommended crates and descriptions
Core semantic and runtime crates
mlpl-core
Description:
Core language data types, symbols, errors, spans, and shared semantic definitions.

Purpose:
This is the stable center. No evaluator logic, no parsing, no UI logic. Just the shared model.

mlpl-array
Description:
Dense array and tensor types, shapes, indexing, views, layout, and structural operations.

Purpose:
This is your tensor substrate. It should be usable independently of the language parser.

Why important:
You want ML experiments and visualizations. This crate becomes the engine for:

shape/rank demos

matrix ops

layer visualization

possible direct Rust usage outside the language

mlpl-runtime
Description:
Runtime values, environments, namespaces, boxing, and execution support.

Purpose:
Holds the dynamic value model:

scalars

arrays

boxed/nested arrays

functions

namespaces/modules

maybe future model/layer objects

mlpl-parser
Description:
Lexer, parser, syntax tree, and source handling for MLPL.

Purpose:
Keep syntax concerns isolated from semantics.

Recommendation:
Start with ASCII-first input, with optional Unicode aliases later.

mlpl-eval
Description:
Interpreter and execution engine for MLPL expressions, functions, and programs.

Purpose:
Implements:

primitive dispatch

rank/cell semantics

broadcasting/pervasion

reductions/scans

function application

namespace resolution

Introspection and visualization crates
mlpl-trace
Description:
Structured execution traces for debugging, animation, and visual explanation.

Purpose:
This is one of the most important design decisions for your project.

Every meaningful operation should be able to emit:

input values or summaries

output values or summaries

shape/rank info

axis mappings

evaluation steps

timing/metadata

This makes the later Yew app much easier.

mlpl-viz
Description:
Shared visualization models and render helpers for arrays, tensors, traces, and graphs.

Purpose:
A Rust crate that defines visual projections such as:

grid view

tree view

axis view

heatmap metadata

histogram/bar metadata

trace timeline model

This allows the CLI and WASM UI to share concepts.

mlpl-wasm
Description:
WebAssembly-facing bindings and adapters for MLPL execution and visualization.

Purpose:
Bridge layer between core crates and Yew frontend.

ML integration crate
mlpl-ml
Description:
Machine learning integrations, tensor backends, layers, optimizers, and experiments.

Purpose:
This is where you can explore:

Burn adapters

Candle adapters

simple neural net demos

training loops

visualization of parameters/gradients

experimentation with papers like Engram, mHC, RLM-inspired structures

Important design note:
Do not let this crate own the language semantics. It should extend the platform, not define it.

Tooling crates/apps
mlpl-cli
Description:
Command-line tooling for running files, evaluating expressions, inspecting arrays, and exporting traces.

Purpose:
This is the user-facing command surface.

mlpl-lsp
Description:
Language tooling support for MLPL editors and IDE integrations.

Purpose:
Optional later. Good if you want AI agents and editors to interact more richly.

apps/mlpl-web
Description:
Yew/WASM visual environment for stepping through MLPL programs and exploring tensors.

apps/mlpl-lab
Description:

MLPL Monorepo PRD
1) Product definition
Product name
MLPL — Machine Learning Programming Language

Product thesis
MLPL is a Rust-first array and tensor programming language platform inspired by APL, APL2, J, and BQN, designed for:

learning by implementing language ideas

visualizing execution, tensors, and transformations

experimenting with machine learning systems in Rust

serving as a realistic multi-agent software project for AI coordination

Primary product outputs
MLPL is not one binary. It is a platform with several outputs:

a Rust CLI / REPL

a Rust/Yew/WASM visual app

a Rust library ecosystem

a demo and tutorial corpus

a blog/video teaching pipeline

Primary user personas
Persona A: the builder-teacher
Wants to learn array-language concepts by implementing them and explaining them visually.

Persona B: the Rust ML experimenter
Wants a tensor-friendly language and runtime for prototyping ideas without defaulting to Python.

Persona C: the AI-orchestrator
Wants a realistic codebase for coordinating multiple coding agents with bounded tasks and measurable outputs.

2) Goals
Primary goals
Build a clean-room Rust implementation of a compact tensor/array language.

Make the implementation visually explainable.

Design the runtime to support ML-oriented experiments.

Enable strong decomposition into independently implementable crates and milestones.

Secondary goals
Support eventual IDE/editor tooling.

Support optional Unicode syntax without requiring it.

Allow visual, not just textual, inspection of program behavior.

Non-goals for early phases
Full compatibility with APL, J, BQN, or Dyalog

Full optimizer/autograd stack from day one

Distributed runtime

GPU support in the first milestone

JIT compiler in the first milestone

3) Product scope
In scope
parser

interpreter

dense arrays / tensors

rank/cell-oriented operations

structured tracing

visual debugger foundation

later: boxed/nested arrays

later: namespaces/modules

later: ML adapters and experiments

Out of scope initially
full compiler to native code

sophisticated optimizer passes

full symbolic algebra

production-grade distributed ML training

4) Success criteria
Technical success
Can execute non-trivial tensor/array programs correctly.

Can emit structured traces suitable for visualization.

Can demonstrate rank/cell semantics visually.

Can run at least one simple ML demo end-to-end.

Educational success
Can explain key APL-family ideas through the implementation.

Can show side-by-side “before/after” evolution from simple arrays to richer semantics.

Can support a blog/video series with compelling demos.

Process success
Can be decomposed into small TDD-friendly work items.

Can be implemented by a coordinator plus several coding agents.

Each milestone results in a visible, demonstrable artifact.

Architecture
1) High-level architecture
MLPL should be split into five conceptual layers.

Layer A — Language definition
syntax

AST

source spans

primitive catalog

function definitions

module/namespace structure

Layer B — Runtime semantics
values

arrays/tensors

boxed/nested values

function application

environments

rank/cell semantics

primitive evaluation

Layer C — Execution instrumentation
trace events

snapshots / summaries

timing / operation metadata

visual projection metadata

Layer D — User interfaces
CLI / REPL

file runner

web UI

visual debugger

future IDE/LSP

Layer E — ML experimentation
tensor backend abstraction

optional adapters to Burn/Candle/dfdx

visualizations of layers, weights, activations, gradients

experiment harnesses

2) Architectural style
Recommended style
Use a library-first monorepo with thin apps.

That means:

most logic lives in reusable crates

apps/ are integration shells

core types are shared and stable

visual and ML features build on traceable runtime semantics

Why this is best
It matches your constraints:

TDD-friendly

easy agent decomposition

supports CLI and WASM without duplicated logic

makes it easier to later spin pieces into separate repos if needed

3) Nonfunctional requirements
Maintainability
small crates

small modules

simple interfaces

stable data contracts between layers

Testability
pure functions wherever possible

deterministic traces

golden test support

property tests for array identities and shape invariants

Explainability
every meaningful operation should be traceable

visual UI should not reverse-engineer behavior; it should consume explicit runtime metadata

Performance
enough for demos and modest ML experiments

avoid premature optimization in early phases

design APIs so internals can later optimize storage/layout

Portability
Rust-native

CLI on Linux/macOS first

WASM frontend later

no Python dependency in core workflows

Crate boundary plan
Below is the recommended monorepo structure.

mlpl/
  Cargo.toml
  README.md
  LICENSE-MIT
  LICENSE-APACHE
  docs/
  examples/
  demos/
  blog/
  apps/
  crates/
Core crates
mlpl-core
Purpose: foundational shared definitions

Contains:

spans

identifiers

symbol names

primitive IDs

language-level error enums

shared metadata types

Should not contain:

parser logic

evaluator logic

UI logic

mlpl-array
Purpose: dense arrays/tensors and shape/layout logic

Contains:

scalar element enum or typed storage abstractions

shape

rank

strides/layout metadata

views/slices

reshape/transpose/index helpers

broadcast compatibility checks

Key design decision:
Keep this crate useful independently of the language.
It should feel like a reusable Rust tensor substrate.

mlpl-runtime
Purpose: runtime values and environments

Contains:

Value

boxed/nested values later

function values

namespace/module values later

environment/frame structure

runtime errors

display/summary helpers for values

Separation principle:
mlpl-array knows arrays.
mlpl-runtime knows language values.

mlpl-parser
Purpose: syntax handling

Contains:

lexer

parser

AST

source-file representation

parse diagnostics

Early recommendation:
Use ASCII syntax first.
Add Unicode aliases later.

mlpl-eval
Purpose: interpreter

Contains:

AST evaluation

primitive dispatch

rank/cell application rules

reductions/scans

function call semantics

environment lookup

module/namespace resolution later

Separation principle:
Interpreter logic here; representation logic elsewhere.

Introspection and visualization crates
mlpl-trace
Purpose: structured execution trace model

Contains:

operation events

input/output summaries

span references

value IDs

shape/rank metadata

timeline/event grouping

serialization format

This crate is strategically critical.
It is what turns MLPL from a language project into a visual teaching platform.

mlpl-viz
Purpose: visual projection helpers

Contains:

grid projection models

tree projection models

tensor heatmap metadata

axis labeling metadata

trace timeline projection helpers

Important:
This is not the UI itself.
This crate defines the reusable visual model.

mlpl-wasm
Purpose: WASM bridge adapters

Contains:

wrappers around evaluator + trace

serialization/adapters for web UI

JS/WASM boundary-safe types if needed

ML crate
mlpl-ml
Purpose: machine learning features and experiments

Contains:

initial pure-Rust demo layers

simple training loops

visualizable model state

optional adapters to Burn/Candle/dfdx later

paper experiments

Key rule:
This crate must depend on the language/runtime, not own it.

Tooling crates
mlpl-cli
Purpose: command-line interface and runner

Contains:

CLI arg parsing

file execution

expression evaluation

trace export

simple REPL shell support, or delegate to app

mlpl-lsp
Purpose: future editor tooling

Contains later:

diagnostics

hover/help

primitive docs

trace-aware inspection hooks

This can wait until well after MVP.

Apps
apps/mlpl-repl
Interactive shell for:

evaluating expressions

loading files

toggling trace output

inspecting values

apps/mlpl-web
Yew/WASM UI for:

stepping through traces

visualizing arrays/tensors

inspecting function applications

later viewing model/layer state

apps/mlpl-lab
Post-MVP experiment workbench for:

demos

notebooks/lab-style workflows

ML experiment panels

paper prototype dashboards

Language design recommendation
Core semantic strategy
Make the runtime:

tensor-first

rank-aware

traceable

extensible toward boxed/nested arrays

Surface syntax strategy
Start with:

ASCII-first

compact but readable

Unicode pretty-print later

avoid over-optimizing for historical fidelity early

Semantic priorities in order
dense arrays/tensors

elementwise operations

shape/rank

broadcasting/pervasion

reductions/scans

rank/cell semantics

boxed/nested arrays

namespaces/modules

optional tacit/combinator features

Testing methodology
Testing pyramid
Level 1: unit tests
For:

shapes

indexing

parser tokens

AST formation

primitive semantics

environment lookup

Level 2: property tests
For:

reshape invariants

transpose properties

rank preservation rules

serialization round-trips

parser/pretty-printer round-trips where applicable

Level 3: golden tests
For:

CLI output

trace output

visual projection JSON

demo programs

Level 4: scenario tests
For:

full demo scripts

REPL interactions

web trace fixture rendering

simple ML experiment runs

Suggested AI-agent decomposition
You mentioned using an AI coordinator and multiple coding agents. This project fits that very well.

Good agent lanes
Lane A — language front end

AST

Lane B — tensor substrate
shapes

Lane C — evaluator

Each lane can work largely independently if the contracts are written first.

Suggested TDD rhythm for each milestone
For each saga step, use this loop:

Step 1
Write acceptance demo scenarios first.

Step 2
Write unit tests for smallest core behavior.

Step 3
Write golden outputs for CLI or trace formats.

Step 4
Implement minimal passing code.

Step 5
Refactor toward clearer module boundaries.

Step 6
Record a tiny demo and update milestone docs.

That last step matters.
A milestone is stronger when it leaves behind:

code

tests

docs

demo artifact

Recommended MVP statement
Here is the crisp MVP definition I would use in the repo.

MLPL MVP is a Rust-first tensor/array language with a CLI and a browser-based visual trace viewer. It supports dense arrays, basic tensor operations, broadcasting, reductions, and rank/cell semantics, and can explain execution step-by-step visually.

That is specific, demonstrable, and strong.

Final recommendation
The best path is:

build tensor-first

make traceability a first-class feature

reach rank/cell semantics by MVP

add APL2-style boxed/nested arrays after MVP

then build upward into ML experiments and research prototypes

This sequencing gives you:

a clean teaching narrative

a realistic agent-orchestration project

meaningful demos at every stage

a strong eventual platform for ML research experiments in Rust

If you want, next I can turn this directly into:
1. a concrete docs/prd.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/saga.md set, or
2. a proposed Cargo workspace tree with per-crate responsibilities and first issue backlog.


proceed, but also consider: each agent can get overloaded with info/context while it is trying to implement a small incremental part.  How can we compartmentalize such that each agent only sees the contract it is expected to test/implement.  Is there a way to structure the mono-repo so that each agent starts in a subdirectory with very limited context (additional info from other dirs only provided if and when needed.

Yes. The right way to handle this is to design the monorepo not just for code reuse, but for context isolation.

Your added requirement changes the implementation plan in an important way:

the repo should support small, contract-first work packets

most agents should begin inside a single subdirectory

they should see only:

their local crate

a tiny contract crate or spec file

a few acceptance tests

cross-crate details should be pulled in only on demand

That argues for a cellular monorepo rather than a “big shared workspace with everybody reading everything” design.

Revised design goals
We now want the monorepo to optimize for all of these:

Product goals
MLPL language/runtime/UI/ML platform

Engineering goals
TDD

demonstrable milestones

clean crate boundaries

Agent-orchestration goals
strict compartmentalization

local contracts

minimal starting context

explicit dependency promotion when needed

limited file footprint per task

Core idea: a cellular monorepo
Instead of treating the monorepo as one giant codebase, treat it as a collection of cells.

Each cell should have:

its own README.md

its own AGENTS.md or WORK_CONTRACT.md

its own tests

its own public contract

as few dependencies as possible

no need to inspect unrelated directories

The coordinator sees the big picture.
Most coding agents see only one cell.

Recommended monorepo structure
Here is the revised top-level structure I recommend.

mlpl/
  README.md
  AGENTS.md
  Cargo.toml
  Cargo.lock
  justfile
  docs/
    prd.md
    architecture.md
    saga.md
    repo-structure.md
    agent-coordination.md
    milestone-mvp.md
  contracts/
    array-contract/
    parser-contract/
    runtime-contract/
    eval-contract/
    trace-contract/
    viz-contract/
    ml-contract/
  crates/
    mlpl-core/
    mlpl-array/
    mlpl-parser/
    mlpl-runtime/
    mlpl-eval/
    mlpl-trace/
    mlpl-viz/
    mlpl-wasm/
    mlpl-ml/
    mlpl-cli/
  apps/
    mlpl-repl/
    mlpl-web/
    mlpl-lab/
  demos/
  examples/
  scripts/
  tools/
The new element here is the contracts/ directory.

That is the main mechanism for keeping agent context small.

How the compartmentalization works
Principle 1: agents implement against contracts, not the whole repo
For example:

mlpl-array agents should mostly read:

contracts/array-contract/

crates/mlpl-array/

mlpl-parser agents should mostly read:

contracts/parser-contract/

crates/mlpl-parser/

mlpl-trace agents should mostly read:

contracts/trace-contract/

crates/mlpl-trace/

This reduces context dramatically.

Principle 2: contracts are tiny and human-written
Each contract package should contain only:

a short purpose statement

public types/interfaces

invariants

examples

acceptance tests or fixtures

dependency assumptions

It should not contain long architectural essays.

Think of each contract as a mini RFC plus executable tests.

Principle 3: one-way dependency flow
To keep context minimal, dependencies should mostly flow one direction:

core
  ↓
array
  ↓
runtime
  ↓
eval
  ↓
trace
  ↓
viz / wasm / apps
And:

array + runtime + eval
  ↓
ml
Avoid cycles.
Avoid “everyone imports everyone.”

That way each agent can understand its local upstream contracts without having to understand downstream consumers.

Recommended directory-local control files
To support agents with minimal context, each major directory should have a local control file.

At repo root
AGENTS.md
This is for the coordinator and human owner.
It defines:

overall mission

dependency graph

coding rules

escalation rules

how an agent asks for more context

when an agent must stop and hand off

Most implementation agents should not need to read much beyond the first page.

In each crate
crates/mlpl-array/AGENTS.md
Example contents:

crate purpose

what this crate is allowed to know

what it must not import

public API summary

local testing commands

local invariants

current milestone scope

This is the primary context for the agent assigned there.

In each contract package
contracts/array-contract/README.md
Contains:

public contract

examples

boundary cases

expected error behavior

This is even smaller than a crate-level AGENTS.md.

Recommended contract package design
Each contract directory should look like this:

contracts/array-contract/
  README.md
  API.md
  INVARIANTS.md
  EXAMPLES.md
  fixtures/
  tests/
For some contracts, API.md and INVARIANTS.md can be merged.

Example: contracts/array-contract/README.md
It should contain only things like:

shape model

indexing semantics

reshape rules

transpose rules

errors

no language syntax

no web UI details

no ML backend discussion

That keeps the array agent focused.

Example: contracts/trace-contract/README.md
It should contain only:

what an event is

required fields

ordering guarantees

value-summary contract

stable serialization requirements

fixture examples

The trace agent should not need parser internals or web rendering internals.

Strong recommendation: use public test crates for contracts
This is one of the best techniques for context isolation.

Instead of relying only on prose docs, create contract test crates that depend on the implementation crate.

Example:

contracts/array-contract/tests/
  shape_contract.rs
  reshape_contract.rs
  transpose_contract.rs
These tests encode the contract.
The implementation agent works until those tests pass.

This gives you:

unambiguous scope

TDD support

reduced need for narrative context

reusable acceptance criteria

How to keep agents from wandering the repo
There are several practical tactics.

1. Start each agent in a subdirectory
When possible, initialize an agent in:

crates/mlpl-array/

or contracts/array-contract/

not at repo root.

That alone reduces accidental context acquisition.

2. Give each agent a local task packet
Each packet should include only:

task statement

allowed directories

required tests

prohibited dependencies

escalation triggers

Example:

Task: Implement reshape validation for mlpl-array.
Allowed dirs:
  - crates/mlpl-array
  - contracts/array-contract
Do not inspect:
  - crates/mlpl-eval
  - apps/*
Success:
  - reshape_contract.rs passes
  - no API changes outside mlpl-array
Escalate if:
  - contract seems inconsistent
  - new error type required in mlpl-core
This is much better than dumping architecture docs into every task.

3. Make “upstream only” the default read rule
An agent may read:

its own crate

contract crate

direct upstream crates only if necessary

An agent may not read:

downstream UI

unrelated demos

future milestones

blog/docs unless specifically needed

That keeps mental load low.

4. Prefer narrow facade crates and summary types
For example, mlpl-trace should receive value summaries, not whole runtime internals.

Bad:

trace agent must understand full Value internals

Better:

runtime exposes ValueSummary

trace consumes ValueSummary

This reduces coupling and context size.

Recommended crate API style for low-context agents
To make this work, APIs need to be intentionally narrow.

Prefer:
small structs

small enums

pure functions

no deep hidden side effects

explicit error types

explicit conversion/adaptation layers

Avoid:
giant “god” modules

big prelude exports

deep macro magic early

broad trait hierarchies too soon

runtime globals

Those increase the amount of context an agent must hold.

Recommended “capsule” design per crate
Each crate should behave like a capsule.

Each capsule should have:
1. README.md
One-screen overview.

Small modules only.

5. tests/
Local tests.

6. examples/
Tiny examples.

Local progress.

That way an agent can operate inside a capsule without browsing the world.

Recommended policy for information escalation
You asked whether additional info can be provided only if and when needed.

Yes. You should make that explicit.

Standard escalation ladder
Level 0 — local only
Agent gets:

current crate

local contract

local tests

local milestone note

Level 1 — direct upstream contract
If blocked, agent may read:

contract of direct upstream crate

public API of upstream crate

Level 2 — coordinator summary
If still blocked, coordinator provides:

small textual summary of needed external constraint

not whole files

Level 3 — selective file reveal
Only if necessary:

one or two exact files outside local scope

Level 4 — design review / human decision
If contract mismatch or architecture conflict exists

This keeps context creep under control.

Strong recommendation: define “what not to know”
This is underused, but very effective.

Each crate AGENTS.md should explicitly say:

Example for mlpl-array
This crate does not need to know:

parser syntax

evaluator rules

trace serialization

Yew UI structure

ML backend adapters

That keeps agents from inventing unnecessary coupling.

My final recommendation
Yes, you should absolutely structure the monorepo so that each agent starts in a subdirectory with very limited context.

The best way to do that is:
1. Use a cellular monorepo
Small crate capsules with local docs and tests.

2. Add a contracts/ layer
Each implementation crate works against a tiny local contract package.

3. Enforce a one-way dependency graph
Upstream-only visibility by default.

4. Give agents task packets
Allowed dirs, prohibited dirs, done criteria, escalation rules.

5. Require context escalation in stages
Local first, then upstream contract, then coordinator summary, then selective reveal.

6. Make trace, viz, and ML consume summaries
Not full internals, unless absolutely needed.

Best practical shape for MLPL

Build MLPL as a contract-first cellular monorepo where each crate is a small capsule, each capsule has its own local agent instructions, and most work is done against tiny contract test suites without browsing the full repository.

That will help both your human workflow and your AI-agent dogfooding goals.

should each agent work in a branch named after it and the feature, use pull requests, and/or use git-worktrees?  what is a simple reliable way to reduce friction and interference between agents.  Another agent can deal with merge conflicts, which should be rare.

Yes. For your use case, I would use all three, but with very different roles:

git worktrees for isolation and low friction

branches for ownership and review boundaries

pull requests as the integration contract

The simple reliable default is:

Recommended baseline
One agent = one worktree + one branch + one small PR
That gives you the best balance of:

isolation

reproducibility

low interference

easy cleanup

clear ownership

Short answer up front
For a coordinator with multiple coding agents working on a monorepo, the cleanest low-friction setup is:

Use this workflow
Create a dedicated branch per agent task

Check that branch out into a dedicated git worktree

Let the agent work only inside its assigned subdirectory/capsule

Require the agent to produce a small PR

Have a separate integration/merge agent or coordinator handle rebases, conflict resolution, and final merge

That is the simplest reliable model I’d recommend.

Why this is better than the alternatives
Option A: branches only, one shared working tree
This is too fragile.

Problems
agents can stomp on each other’s working directory state

switching branches is annoying and error-prone

easy to lose track of uncommitted changes

high friction for parallel work

Verdict
Not recommended for multi-agent parallel work.

Option B: worktrees without disciplined branch naming
Better, but still messy.

Problems
isolation is good, but ownership becomes unclear

hard to know which worktree corresponds to which task

difficult to audit later

Verdict
Useful, but incomplete.

Option C: long-lived agent branches with direct merges to main
This increases drift and integration pain.

Problems
long-lived branches accumulate conflicts

agents may unknowingly diverge from current contracts

integration becomes harder, not easier

Verdict
Avoid long-lived feature branches unless they are intentionally reserved for major epics.

Option D: short-lived task branches in dedicated worktrees, merged via PR
This is the sweet spot.

Benefits
each agent has filesystem isolation

branch ownership is explicit

PR is the review/integration boundary

conflicts are localized

cleanup is easy

Verdict
Recommended.

Recommended repo workflow architecture
Given your “cellular monorepo” idea, I’d structure the git workflow around task-sized slices, not around people or large epics.

Branch naming
Use branch names based on:

milestone/saga

capsule/crate

task ID

optional agent label

Suggested format
feat/<area>/<task-id>-<short-name>
fix/<area>/<task-id>-<short-name>
spike/<area>/<task-id>-<short-name>
Examples:

feat/array/array-reshape-001
feat/parser/parser-lex-001
feat/trace/trace-event-001
fix/eval/eval-broadcast-002
spike/ml/ml-backend-burn-001
If you want agent identity in the branch name, append it at the end, not the beginning:

feat/array/array-reshape-001-agent-a
That keeps branches grouped by feature area rather than by agent.

Should the branch be named after the agent?
My recommendation
No, not primarily.
Name it after the task, with optional agent suffix.

Why
Agents are transient. Tasks are durable.

You will care later about:

what feature the branch implemented

what contract it touched

what milestone it belonged to

You will care less about:

whether “agent-3” or “agent-blue” wrote it

So prefer:

feat/array/array-reshape-001-agent-blue
over:

agent-blue/reshape
Should each agent get a worktree?
Yes
For your scenario, worktrees are the cleanest operational isolation mechanism.

Why worktrees help
Each agent gets:

its own directory

its own checked-out branch

its own build artifacts if you configure target dirs locally

no branch-switching friction

no accidental overwrites of another agent’s working directory

Example layout
~/dev/mlpl-main
~/dev/mlpl-wt-array-reshape-001
~/dev/mlpl-wt-parser-lex-001
~/dev/mlpl-wt-trace-event-001
That is much better than trying to multiplex agents in one checkout.

Should you use pull requests?
Yes, absolutely
PRs should be the integration boundary, even if the reviewer is another agent.

Why PRs matter
They provide:

a natural review point

a visible diff

a place to attach task/contract metadata

CI gatekeeping

a clean handoff to a merge/conflict-resolution agent

For your use case, a PR is less about human social workflow and more about state transition control.

Think of it as:

implementation done → validated artifact ready for integration

Best simple reliable setup
If you want the minimum-friction reliable process, use this:

Core rule
Task branch + dedicated worktree + small PR
That’s it.

Then add these constraints:

Constraints
one task per branch

one branch per worktree

one PR per task

keep PRs small

merge quickly

delete branch and remove worktree after merge

That will keep conflicts rare.

Recommended operational model
1. Coordinator owns main
Only the coordinator or merge agent integrates into main.

2. Each coding agent gets a task packet
The packet includes:

task ID

allowed directories

contract files

target branch name

target worktree path

3. Agent works in isolated worktree
No branch switching. No cross-task editing.

4. Agent opens PR when local tests pass
PR should reference:

task ID

contract

milestone

allowed dirs

5. Integration agent handles:
rebasing

conflict resolution

merge

branch/worktree cleanup

This is very close to the simplest stable system.

Recommended branch / PR / worktree conventions
Branch naming
Use:

feat/<area>/<task-id>-<slug>
fix/<area>/<task-id>-<slug>
docs/<area>/<task-id>-<slug>
Examples:

feat/array/array-shape-001-shape-type
feat/array/array-reshape-002-reshape-op
feat/parser/parser-lex-001-basic-lexer
feat/eval/eval-arith-001-scalar-arith
feat/trace/trace-event-001-core-events
Worktree naming
Mirror the branch, but filesystem-friendly:

wt-array-shape-001
wt-array-reshape-002
wt-parser-lex-001
wt-trace-event-001
Or place them under a dedicated folder:

../mlpl-worktrees/array-shape-001
../mlpl-worktrees/parser-lex-001
PR title format
[array] ARRAY-SHAPE-001: add Shape type and invariants
[parser] PARSER-LEX-001: implement numeric/token lexer
[trace] TRACE-EVENT-001: add core trace event model
This makes browsing and automation easier.

How to reduce friction and interference
You asked for a simple reliable way to reduce friction and interference.

Here is the lowest-friction recipe I recommend.

Friction-reduction recipe
Rule 1: Keep tasks narrow
Each branch should touch:

one crate

one contract

maybe one shared core file only if explicitly allowed

Avoid multi-crate tasks early.

Rule 2: Prefer upstream-first sequencing
Have agents work in this order:

core

array/parser

runtime

eval

trace

viz/web

ml

That reduces “everybody blocked on everything.”

Rule 3: Use tiny PRs
Small PRs merge faster and conflict less.

Ideal size:

1 focused change

1 contract

1 acceptance test set

Avoid “week-long mega branches.”

Rule 4: Merge early, rebase rarely
Do not let branches age.

Preferred flow:

open task

implement

run tests

PR

integrate quickly

Short branch lifetime is one of the strongest conflict reducers.

Rule 5: One merge agent handles conflicts
You already suggested this. I agree.

That agent should:

rebase or merge from main

resolve conflicts

run integration tests

push updated branch

merge PR

remove worktree and branch

This keeps coding agents focused on implementation.

Rule 6: Separate build output per worktree when practical
This avoids odd interference and makes cleanup easier.

For example, use a local target dir:

CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target-local
or per-worktree target dirs if needed.

This is especially helpful if many agents build in parallel.

Rule 7: Forbid unrelated edits
Each task packet should explicitly say:

allowed dirs

forbidden dirs

whether shared files may be touched

This matters more than git itself for reducing interference.

Suggested integration model
Here’s a practical model that matches your needs.

Roles
1. Coordinator
chooses task order

creates task packets

creates worktrees/branches

assigns agents

2. Coding agent
works only in assigned worktree

implements contract

opens PR

3. Merge/integration agent
rebases/merges branch

resolves conflicts

runs cross-crate tests

merges PR

deletes branch

removes worktree

This is clean and scalable.

Suggested commands / workflow example
Create a task branch and worktree
From repo root:

git fetch origin
git checkout main
git pull --ff-only

git worktree add ../mlpl-worktrees/array-reshape-001 -b feat/array/array-reshape-001 origin/main
Then the agent works inside:

../mlpl-worktrees/array-reshape-001
When done:

git status
cargo test -p mlpl-array
git add ...
git commit -m "ARRAY-RESHAPE-001: implement reshape validation and op"
git push -u origin feat/array/array-reshape-001
Open PR.

After merge:

git worktree remove ../mlpl-worktrees/array-reshape-001
git branch -d feat/array/array-reshape-001
git push origin --delete feat/array/array-reshape-001
That is low-friction and repeatable.

Should you use merge commits, squash, or rebase?
For your workflow, I recommend:

PR merge style
Squash merge for most task branches
Why squash helps
keeps history cleaner

one task = one logical commit on main

reduces noise from agent intermediate commits

easier to bisect milestone-level changes later

Use commit message format tied to task ID:

ARRAY-RESHAPE-001: implement reshape validation and op
Exception
Use normal merge only if preserving branch commit history is important for a larger epic.

But for most agent tasks:
squash merge is best.

Should you allow stacked branches?
Early on: no
Stacked branches create extra dependency complexity and more context burden.

Prefer:

merge prerequisite task

then spawn downstream task from updated main

Later, if the system is mature, you can allow stack-based development for larger epics, but not at the beginning.

Recommended simplest reliable policy
If I had to boil it down to one operational policy for MLPL, it would be this:

Policy
Every task:
starts from current main

gets a dedicated short-lived branch

gets a dedicated worktree

stays inside allowed directories

ends in a small PR

is squash-merged by an integration agent

That is the simplest reliable system I know for reducing friction and interference.

What should be rare
You said merge conflicts should be rare. I agree. To make that true:

Make these rare
multi-crate edits

long-lived branches

shared-file edits

simultaneous editing of the same contract

ad hoc refactors

touching root workspace files unless necessary

The main remaining conflict hotspots will be:

workspace Cargo.toml

lockfiles

shared docs

shared contract files

root README

Those should usually be handled by the coordinator or merge agent, not by every coding agent.

Recommended ownership of shared files
To reduce conflicts further, assign ownership:

Coordinator / merge agent owns:
root Cargo.toml

Cargo.lock

root README.md

top-level docs

workspace-wide CI config

Crate agents own:
their crate files

their crate tests

their local README/AGENTS/MILESTONE files

their contract tests if assigned

This is a big conflict reducer.

Final recommendation
Yes:

Use
one short-lived branch per task

one dedicated git worktree per branch

one small PR per task

Prefer
branch names based on feature/task, optionally with agent suffix

squash merges

a separate merge/integration agent

strict allowed directory boundaries

quick merges and cleanup

Avoid
shared working trees

long-lived branches

direct commits to main

broad multi-crate tasks

every agent touching root files

That is the simplest reliable low-friction setup for your multi-agent monorepo.
